title: Applying a timber deck oil and preservative
subtitle: Clean, prepare, and oil a softwood or hardwood deck to protect it from moisture, UV, and biological growth
excerpt: How to apply deck oil and wood preservative to an outdoor timber deck. Covers cleaning the deck surface, sanding or brushing off old finish, applying a penetrating deck oil in the direction of the grain, and the maintenance schedule.
sourceNotes: UK decking maintenance practice. Ronseal, Cuprinol, and Osmo generic application guidance (referenced principles, not brand instructions). Timber trade technical notes on hardwood and softwood deck maintenance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A penetrating oil finish applied to outdoor timber decks. Made from linseed, tung, or other drying oils, often combined with UV-blocking pigments and fungicide to prevent greying, mould, and algae growth. Penetrates into the wood surface rather than forming a film, so it does not crack or peel as film-based deck paints do. Hardwood decks (ipe, teak, hardwood composite) need a penetrating oil formulated for dense hardwood (softwood oils will not penetrate as deeply. Reapply when the surface no longer beads water.

content.text: protects outdoor timber from rain, UV, and biological growth by feeding the surface fibres rather than coating them. The approach is cleaning the deck to bare wood, sanding lightly, applying the oil with a brush in the direction of the grain, and wiping off any surplus. Spring is the best time) the wood is drying after winter and the surface will absorb oil well before summer heat closes the grain.

Sweep off loose debris and leaves. Apply a deck cleaner, mould killer, or a dilute bleach solution (1:10 bleach in water) to the surface. Scrub along the grain with a stiff brush to break up algae, mould, and the grey weathered surface layer. Leave for the contact time on the product label, then rinse thoroughly with a hose. Allow the deck to dry fully (at least 48 hours of dry weather) before oiling. Oiling wet wood traps moisture in and the oil will not penetrate.

If the deck boards have heavy greying or old peeling coatings, sand along the grain with 80-grit on a pole sander or belt sander before oiling. On a fresh deck or lightly weathered deck, a scrub brush and cleaner is sufficient. Sand the cut ends of boards to their full depth (end grain absorbs far more oil than face grain and needs extra attention.

Stir the oil thoroughly) the UV-blocking pigment settles. Apply generously along the grain with a 50 mm brush, working the oil into the gaps between boards. Apply to end grain first with extra passes. The goal is even penetration: the surface should look uniformly darker but not standing in pools. Once applied, wipe any surplus standing on the surface after 15 to 20 minutes with a dry lint-free cloth (surplus that is not absorbed will dry tacky. Apply a second coat the same day while the first is still slightly wet on most softwood decks; allow 4 to 6 hours between coats on hardwood.

Re-oil when the surface no longer beads water) typically annually on softwood, every 2 years on dense hardwood. Clean before each re-oiling cycle. Do not allow algae growth to persist between treatments; it lifts and stains the grain.
